description: The provided image is severely blurred and completely unreadable, no valid experimental or simulation content can be directly identified from visual observation. The associated research studies a Fe-30Mn-9Al-1C-1V-5Ni duplex lightweight steel fabricated via homogenization at 1050 °C for 1 h, hot rolling with 85% total reduction, cold rolling with 50% total reduction, and solution treatment at 900 °C for 15 min followed by water quenching. The steel has a duplex microstructure composed of 78 vol.% austenite and 20 vol.% B2 intermetallic phase, with dual nanoprecipitates of VC (19.9±6.2 nm) and L'1₂-type long-range ordered domains (1.0±0.3 nm) uniformly distributed in the austenite matrix. It achieves an excellent mechanical property combination of 1316±16 MPa yield strength, 1458±11 MPa ultimate tensile strength, and 11.7±1.2% elongation at room temperature, attributed to synergistic strengthening from heterogeneous deformation induced effect, precipitation strengthening, dislocation strengthening, and solid solution strengthening.

This figure consists of two subplots for the Fe-30Mn-9Al-1C-1V-5Ni duplex lightweight steel. Subplot (a) illustrates the full thermomechanical processing route: homogenization at 1050 °C for 1 h, followed by hot rolling with 85% total thickness reduction, then cold rolling with 50% total thickness reduction, solution treatment at 900 °C for 15 min, and final water quenching. Subplot (b) presents the Thermo-Calc calculated equilibrium phase volume fractions as a function of temperature from 500 °C to 1100 °C. It shows austenite volume fraction reaches ~78% at 900 °C, B2 phase maintains ~20% volume fraction at 900 °C, κ-carbide fully dissolves above ~775 °C, while VC retains very low volume fraction across the temperature range. This figure confirms that the 900 °C solution treatment achieves coexistence of austenite, B2 phases and VC precipitates while eliminating brittle κ-carbide, supporting the design of the processing route for good mechanical performance of the steel.

This figure presents XRD and EBSD microstructure characterization of the Fe-30Mn-9Al-1C-1V-5Ni duplex lightweight steel after 900 °C solution treatment followed by water quenching. Subplot (a) is the XRD pattern, confirming the coexistence of austenite (FCC structure), B2 ordered phase, and VC carbide phase, with austenite as the dominant matrix phase. Subplot (b) is the EBSD phase map (scale bar 30 μm), where green represents austenite and red represents B2 phase, with a measured B2 volume fraction of 20%. Subplots (c) and (d) are inverse pole figure (IPF) maps for austenite and B2 phase respectively (both with 30 μm scale bar), showing grain orientation distributions. Subplot (e) shows the Gaussian-fitted grain size frequency distribution of austenite, with an average grain size of 1.31 μm. Subplot (f) shows the bimodal grain size distribution of B2 phase: fine band-shaped B2 grains have an average size of 1.02 μm, while larger particle-shaped B2 grains have an average size of 3.38 μm. The characterization confirms the fine-grained duplex microstructure of the steel, which provides the microstructural basis for its excellent strength-ductility synergy.

This figure presents (S)TEM characterization results of the initial microstructure of Fe-30Mn-9Al-1C-1V-5Ni duplex lightweight steel. Subfigures a1-a8 are STEM-EDS elemental mapping of Fe, Mn, Al, C, V, and Ni, showing elemental distribution and enrichment of Ni and V corresponding to nanoprecipitates. Subfigure b is a high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of the B2/austenite phase interface, with the orientation relationship [011]γ // [111]B2 confirmed by the inset selected area electron diffraction pattern. Subfigure c is an HRTEM image of an individual VC precipitate, with orientation relationship [011]γ // [011]VC verified by the inset diffraction pattern. Subfigure d is a dark-field TEM image captured from the L'1₂ superlattice reflection, showing dispersed L'1₂ nanodomains. Subfigure e is an inverse fast Fourier transform image highlighting L'1₂-type ordered nanodomains. These results confirm the initial microstructure consists of austenite+B2 duplex matrix, plus in-situ precipitated VC nanocarbides and L'1₂ long-range ordered domains in austenite, providing baseline microstructure evidence for subsequent strengthening mechanism analysis.

This figure presents the tensile mechanical properties of the Fe-30Mn-9Al-1C-1V-5Ni duplex lightweight steel. Subplot (a) shows the engineering stress-strain curve of the steel, with a yield strength of ~1300 MPa, ultimate tensile strength of ~1450 MPa, and engineering elongation to failure of ~13%. An inserted SEM fractograph (scale bar 5 μm) in (a) exhibits dense dimples, indicating a typical ductile fracture characteristic. Subplot (b) displays the true stress-strain curve and corresponding work-hardening rate curve, where the work-hardening behavior is divided into three discernible stages: Stage I (0 to ~1% true strain) with a sharp drop in work-hardening rate to below zero, Stage II (~1% to 4.3% true strain) with a rapid increase of work-hardening rate to a peak of ~3.7 GPa, and Stage III (4.3% to 12% true strain) with a gradual decrease but remaining at a high level above 2.7 GPa. An inset in (b) shows the true stress and work-hardening rate variation in the small strain range. This figure confirms that the studied duplex lightweight steel achieves a combination of ultrahigh strength and good ductility, and its three-stage work-hardening behavior corresponds to distinct deformation-induced microstructure evolution mechanisms, supporting the excellent strength-ductility synergy of the material.

This figure presents the Bauschinger effect and heterogeneous deformation induced (HDI) strengthening behavior of the Fe-30Mn-9Al-1C-1V-5Ni duplex lightweight steel, tested via cyclic loading-unloading-reloading (LUR) tensile tests. Subfigure (a) shows the full-range true stress-true strain curve of repeated loading-unloading cycles up to ~11% true strain, with peak true stress reaching ~1750 MPa. Subfigure (b) is the local amplification of a typical hysteresis loop at ~8.6% true strain, which defines unloaded yield stress (σ_u), reloaded yield stress (σ_r), yield drop (Δσ_y) and HDI stress for calculation. Subfigure (c) displays that both hysteresis area (increasing from ~60 MPa to ~205 MPa) and Δσ_y (increasing from ~2 MPa to ~23 MPa) rise monotonically with increasing true strain, reflecting enhanced Bauschinger effect with strain accumulation. Subfigure (d) shows HDI stress increases progressively from ~620 MPa to ~845 MPa as true strain rises from 1% to 11%, which is attributed to geometrically necessary dislocation accumulation at austenite/B2 interfaces. This figure confirms HDI strengthening acts as a critical work-hardening mechanism for the studied duplex steel, supporting its excellent strength-ductility synergy.

This figure presents Geometrically Necessary Dislocation (GND) distribution characterizations of the Fe-30Mn-9Al-1C-1V-5Ni duplex lightweight steel under different tensile strains (0%, 1%, 3%, 6%), derived from electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) measurements. Subfigures (a1)-(d1) show GND mappings of the austenitic phase, while (a2)-(d2) show GND mappings of the B2 phase at each corresponding strain, all with a 5 μm scale bar, and the color bar indicates ρ_GND ranging from 10^13 to 10^16 m^-2. Subfigures (e) and (f) are GND density frequency histograms for austenite and B2 phase, respectively, with Gaussian fitted peak values marked. Subfigure (g) displays the average ρ_GND of both phases as a function of strain. Key trends: Initial GND density of B2 (1.04×10^14 m^-2 at 0% strain) is higher than that of austenite (5.36×10^13 m^-2) due to smaller B2 grain size. Both phases show monotonically increasing GND density with rising strain, and austenite accumulates dislocations more rapidly, surpassing B2 density at 6% strain (2.64×10^14 m^-2 vs 2.51×10^14 m^-2). This confirms co-deformation of the two phases, with higher deformability of austenite, supporting the heterogeneous deformation induced strengthening and strain hardening mechanisms of the duplex steel.

This figure presents electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) derived Schmid factor maps of Fe-30Mn-9Al-1C-1V-5Ni duplex lightweight steel, illustrating the evolution of Schmid factors for two constituent phases under engineering strains of 0%, 1%, 3%, and 6%. Subplots (a1) to (d1) correspond to the face-centered cubic austenite (γ) phase, while (a2) to (d2) correspond to the CsCl-type ordered B2 intermetallic phase. The color bar on the right quantifies Schmid factor values from 0.2 (blue) to 0.5 (red), with a 5 μm scale bar provided for all subplots. The distribution of Schmid factors changes progressively with increasing strain for both phases, and the EBSD data from this figure is used to calculate geometrically necessary dislocation densities, supporting the quantification of dislocation strengthening contributions and revealing the strain-dependent heterogeneous deformation mechanism of the duplex steel.

This figure presents TEM characterization of deformation-induced microstructures in Fe-30Mn-9Al-1C-1V-5Ni duplex lightweight steel subjected to 6% and 12% (near final failure) engineering strains. For the 6% strain sample: (a) BF-TEM image reveals slip bands, dislocation pile-ups around B2 precipitates, and VC nanoprecipitates; (b) DF-TEM image with inset SAED pattern shows weakened long-range ordered (LRO) domains inside slip bands and attenuated superlattice reflections; (c) inverse FFT image confirms reduced LRO domain volume fraction after dislocation shearing; (d) captures VC precipitates impeding dislocation motion. For the 12% strain sample: (e) BF-TEM image exhibits significantly increased dislocation density, dislocation piling-up and bowing out at austenite/B2 interfaces; (f) DF-TEM with SAED shows further attenuated superlattice reflections of LRO domains; (g) inverse FFT confirms further decreased LRO volume fraction; (h) reveals more pronounced VC-dislocation interaction. These results verify the multi-component deformation strengthening mechanisms of the studied steel.

This stacked bar chart quantifies the contribution of different strengthening mechanisms to the yield strength (YS) of the Fe-30Mn-9Al-1C-1V-5Ni duplex lightweight steel, with strength contribution in MPa on the y-axis. The individual strengthening contributions are clearly labeled: heterogeneous deformation induced (HDI) strengthening contributes 597 MPa, austenite lattice friction stress (σ₀,ᵧfᵧ) contributes 78 MPa, austenite solid solution strengthening (σₛₛ,ᵧfᵧ) contributes 182 MPa, austenite dislocation strengthening (σ_dᵢₛ,ᵧfᵧ) contributes 85 MPa, VC precipitation strengthening (σ_VC fᵧ) contributes 74 MPa, L'1₂ long-range order (LRO) strengthening (σ_LRO fᵧ) contributes 119 MPa, B2 phase lattice friction stress (σ₀,B2 f_B2) contributes 135 MPa, B2 phase dislocation strengthening (σ_dᵢₛ,B2 f_B2) contributes 46 MPa. The sum of these contributions matches the experimentally measured yield strength, confirming that HDI strengthening is the dominant strengthening mechanism, and the synergistic effect of multiple strengthening mechanisms leads to the ultrahigh yield strength of the developed duplex lightweight steel.
